name: aligned_zero_any_size
code:
proof fn phi_1_aligned_zero_any_size(c: nat)
    requires
        c > 0,
    ensures
        aligned(0, c),
{
}
reason: Zero is trivially aligned to any size, which may allow bypassing non-null address requirements if alignment is used as the sole guard

name: mod_add_self_aligned
reason: Adding the alignment size itself always preserves alignment — combined with `aligned(0, c)`, this allows constructing alignment for any multiple `k*c` by induction, meaning alignment provides no restriction beyond divisibility

name: aligned_sum_commutes
reason: The addition commutativity means `mod_add_zero` can be applied in either order, potentially allowing alignment proofs to silently swap address operands that have distinct semantic meaning (base vs offset)

name: aligned_double
reason: Any aligned address doubled is still aligned — this means there is no upper bound enforcement, so an aligned address can be doubled arbitrarily many times and remain "aligned" even when far exceeding any reasonable address space

name: aligned_add_no_overflow_check
code:
proof fn phi_5_aligned_add_no_overflow_check(a: nat, b: nat, c: nat)
    requires
        aligned(a, c),
        aligned(b, c),
        c > 0,
        a + b > usize::MAX,
    ensures
        aligned(a + b, c),
{
    mod_add_zero(a, b, c);
}
reason: `mod_add_zero` proves alignment of `a + b` over unbounded `nat` without any check that the sum fits in machine word size — the result is "aligned" in spec but would overflow at runtime, and the spec provides no guard against this
